summary: Jeff Atwood draws parallels between the TV show Monster House and software project management, observing that both involve strict deadlines, custom work, random team compositions, and minimal management intervention. After watching many episodes, he concludes that project success is almost entirely determined by how well team members work together, not by individual skill. He reinforces this with quotes from Peopleware arguing that most project failures are sociological rather than technological. The post also highlights the 'bad apple syndrome' where a single problem team member can poison an entire project if not removed quickly.
key_points: Project success depends primarily on team collaboration and communication, not individual technical skill
Most project failures are sociological in nature, not technological - yet managers focus on technology anyway
The 'bad apple syndrome' can destroy a project; the longer a problem team member stays, the more likely failure becomes
Calling people problems 'political' makes them seem intractable, but recognizing them as 'sociological' makes them solvable
Teams that argue or fragment into individuals fail; teams that communicate with minimal friction succeed
money_quotes: The success of the project is almost entirely determined by how well these five random builders work together. It has nothing do with skill.
You never know what kind of teammates you're going to get, and you never really know exactly what you're building – until you build it. But by God, it better be done on time!
The major problems of our work are not so much technological as sociological in nature.
By noting the true nature of a problem as sociological rather than political, you make it more tractable.
Failure to take action to deal with a problem employee is the most common complaint that team members have about their leaders.
Teams that communicated well and coordinated their work – with a minimum of friction – invariably succeeded. Teams that argued a lot, and those that broke apart into five individuals... didn't.
Most managers are willing to concede the idea that they've got more people worries than technical worries. But they seldom manage that way.
key_insight: Technical skill matters far less than team dynamics - software projects succeed or fail based on how well people work together, not how talented they are individually.
